creator: jau@iki.fi
text:
Solaris-11 also supports F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC which I proposed as a non-standard
extension for FreeBSD...

http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E23824_01/html/821-1463/fcntl-2.html

It may be unnecessary to even mention this, but I see standards as the 
minimum to
what should be supported, not as a sort of maximum that should limit 
what a system
can do.
Limiting the features of a system to copying something which others have 
already
done and documented is simply denying any novel ideas and innovation.
Pushing standards before implementation creates only ISO network stacks 
which
are bound to go the way of the dodo. The Internet model in which the 
successful
implementations drive the standards has clearly shown its strength.
None of this means that people should be somehow lured to using non-standard
features, though. Marking non-standard features clearly as potential 
limitations to
portability is quite worthwhile.

With F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC it will also be very simple to implement dup3() 
(from Linux
and Austin Group) using the existing fcntl() only. This would avoid 
creation of yet another
new system call.

Because old statically linked binaries may depend on old system calls 
every new system
call will have to be supported for a very long time even if they may 
later become only
redundant simplified interfaces for something more generic.
Once something is published at system call level it becomes terribly 
hard to move it
completely back to the user space as a wrapper to something more generic.
 From this point of view implementing dup3() only in the user space 
until such a time
that due to some new requirement it becomes absolutely necessary to push 
it to system
call level would be quite beneficial.
See one potential implementation below.


I hope this helps...
--jau



#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <errno.h>

#if !defined(F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC) && defined(_F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC)
#  define F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC  _F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC
#endif

#if !defined(F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC)
#  error Neither F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC nor _F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC defined!
#endif

#if !defined(O_CLOEXEC)
#  error O_CLOEXEC not defined!
#endif

int
dup3 (oldfd, newfd, how)
     int     oldfd;
     int     newfd;
     int     how;
{

     if (how & ~O_CLOEXEC) {
         errno = EINVAL;
         return (-1);
     }

     how = (how & O_CLOEXEC) ? F_DUP2FD_CLOEXEC : F_DUP2FD;

     return (fcntl (oldfd, how, newfd));
}
